name: pwd
title: Where am I
what: Shows the full path to the current folder (working directory).
when: When you're lost in the file system and need to know which folder you're in.
when_not: To go to another folder — use cd.

name: cd
title: Go to a folder
what: Changes the current working directory — "moves" you into another folder.
when: When you need to enter another folder to work with files there.
when_not: To copy files between folders — use cp.

name: ls
title: List folder contents
what: Lists files and folders in the current (or specified) directory.
when: When you need to see what's in a folder.
when_not: To read a file's contents — use cat or less.

examples.desc: All files, including hidden, with permissions
List files in a specific folder

name: clear
title: Clear the terminal screen
what: Removes all text from the terminal screen for convenience.
when: When the screen is cluttered and you want a fresh start.
when_not: Does not delete command history — it's still there, just not visible.

name: open
title: Open a file or folder in macOS
what: Opens a file or folder in the default app (Finder, browser, etc.).
when: When you need to open a file visually, not through the terminal.
when_not: To read file text in the terminal — use cat or less.

name: cp
title: Copy a file or folder
what: Creates a copy of a file or folder in another location.
when: When you need to copy a file without deleting the original.
when_not: To move files — use mv.

name: mv
title: Move or rename
what: Moves a file/folder to another location or renames it.
when: When you need to move a file or give it a new name.
when_not: To copy while keeping the original — use cp.

name: rm
title: Delete a file or folder
what: Permanently deletes files and folders. There is no Trash in the terminal!
when: When you're sure the file is no longer needed.
when_not: If in doubt — don't delete. A mistake with rm -rf can destroy important data.

examples.desc: Delete a folder and everything inside

danger_note: rm -rf deletes without confirmation and without Trash. Double-check the path!

name: mkdir
title: Create a folder
what: Creates a new directory (folder) with the specified name.
when: When you need to organize files in a new folder.
when_not: To create an empty file — use touch.

name: touch
title: Create an empty file
what: Creates a new empty file or updates the modification time of an existing one.
when: When you need to quickly create an empty file.
when_not: To create a folder — use mkdir.

name: cat
title: Show file contents
what: Prints the entire text of a file to the terminal.
when: For small text files — configs, scripts, notes.
when_not: For large files — use less, otherwise the screen will fill with text.

name: less
title: View a file with scrolling
what: Opens a file for paginated viewing. You can scroll up and down.
when: For large files — logs, long texts.
when_not: For small files, cat is enough.

name: head
title: First lines of a file
what: Shows the beginning of a file (10 lines by default).
when: When you need a quick look at the start — CSV headers, beginning of a log.
when_not: For the end of a file — use tail.

name: tail
title: Last lines of a file
what: Shows the end of a file (10 lines by default).
when: For logs — view recent entries. tail -f watches a file in real time.
when_not: For the beginning of a file — use head.

name: find
title: Find files by name or condition
what: Recursively searches for files and folders in the specified directory.
when: When you know part of a file name but not where it is.
when_not: To search text inside files — use grep.

examples.desc: Find all .txt in current folder and below

name: grep
title: Find text inside files
what: Searches for lines containing the specified text or pattern.
when: When you need to find a word or phrase in files or command output.
when_not: To search for files by name — use find.

examples.desc: Find lines with 'error' in a file
Find 'TODO' in all files in the folder

name: which
title: Where a command is located
what: Shows the full path to a command's executable file.
when: When you need to know which program will run when you type a command.
when_not: To search for files — use find.

name: where
title: All locations of a command
what: Shows all paths where a command is found (in zsh — equivalent to which -a).
when: When which shows one location but you suspect there are multiple versions.
when_not: Usually which is enough.

name: man
title: Full command documentation
what: Opens the official manual for a command. Detailed, but in English.
when: When you need all flags and details not covered in cmd.
when_not: For a quick start — use cmd first, man for deeper reference.

name: history
title: History of entered commands
what: Shows a list of commands you've already entered in this terminal.
when: When you forgot what you typed earlier or want to repeat a command.
when_not: For command reference — use cmd.

name: echo
title: Print text to the terminal
what: Prints the specified text or variable value.
when: To check variables, output in scripts, simple messages.
when_not: Not for reading files — use cat.

name: chmod
title: Change file permissions
what: Changes permissions: who can read, write, and execute a file.
when: When a script won't run — it may lack execute permission.
when_not: Be careful with chmod 777 — it grants full access to everyone.
danger_note: Wrong permissions can make a file inaccessible or insecure.

name: df
title: Free disk space
what: Shows how much space is used and free on disks.
when: When a disk is filling up or you need to check available space.
when_not: For the size of a specific folder — use du.

examples.desc: Disk space in human-readable format (GB, MB)

name: du
title: Folder and file sizes
what: Shows how much space files and folders take up.
when: When you need to find what's using the most space.
when_not: For overall disk space — use df.

examples.desc: Size of each item in current folder

name: curl
title: Download or send data over the network
what: Works with URLs: downloads pages, files, sends API requests.
when: To check a site, download a file, test an API.
when_not: For interactive browsing — open a browser or use open.

name: ping
title: Check host availability
what: Sends packets to a server and shows whether it responds.
when: When you need to check internet or site/server availability.
when_not: Does not diagnose all network issues — only basic connectivity check.

name: ssh
title: Connect to a remote server
what: Opens a secure connection to another computer through the terminal.
when: To work on a remote server, VPS, or another Mac on the network.
when_not: To download files without logging in — use scp or curl.

name: brew
title: Install programs on macOS
what: Homebrew package manager — installs CLI programs with one command.
when: When you need a program not included in macOS by default (fzf, git, node...).
when_not: For GUI apps — brew install --cask or App Store.

name: git
title: Version control system
what: Tracks changes in project files. Lets you save history, roll back, and work in a team.
when: For any code or project where change history matters.
when_not: Not for copying files — that's cp. Git is about versions and history.

name: ps
title: List running processes
what: Shows programs and processes currently running.
when: When you need to see what's running or find a process PID for kill.
when_not: For interactive monitoring — use top or htop.

name: kill
title: Stop a process
what: Terminates a process by its PID (process ID).
when: When a program is frozen and won't close normally.
when_not: Find the PID with ps first. Don't kill processes if you don't know what they are.

examples.desc: Force kill (if regular kill doesn't work)

danger_note: kill -9 cannot be intercepted — the process dies immediately. Use only if regular kill doesn't help.

name: top
title: Real-time process monitoring
what: Shows a live list of processes: CPU, memory, who's using how much.
when: When the computer is slow and you need to find the culprit.
when_not: For a one-time list — ps is enough.
